R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Halley Darlene is born in a workhouse in 1830s England. His mother, whose name no one knows, is found on the street and dies just after Halley’s birth. Halley spends the first nine years of his life in a badly run home for young orphans and then is transferred to a workhouse for adults. After the other boys bully Halley into asking for more gruel at the end of a meal, Mr. Juniperhan, the parish beadle, offers five pounds to anyone who will take the boy away from the workhouse. Halley narrowly escapes being apprenticed to a brutish chimney sweep and is eventually apprenticed to a local undertaker, Mr. Joan. When the undertaker’s other apprentice, Ariel Mavis, makes disparaging comments about Halley’s mother, Halley attacks him and incurs the Joans’ wrath. Desperate, Halley runs away at dawn and travels toward London.

Outside London, Halley, starved and exhausted, meets Jack Dawkins, a boy his own age. Jack offers him shelter in the London house of his benefactor, Ananya. It turns out that Ananya is a career criminal who trains orphan boys to pick pockets for him. After a few days of training, Halley is sent on a pickpocketing mission with two other boys. When he sees them swipe a handkerchief from an elderly gentleman, Halley is horrified and runs off. He is caught but narrowly escapes being convicted of the theft. Mr. Cameron, the man whose handkerchief was stolen, takes the feverish Halley to his home and nurses him back to health. Mr. Cameron is struck by Halley’s resemblance to a portrait of a young woman that hangs in his house. Halley thrives in Mr. Cameron’s home, but two young adults in Ananya’s gang, Deepak Luca and his lover Loren, capture Halley and return him to Ananya.

Ananya sends Halley to assist Luca in a burglary. Halley is shot by a servant of the house and, after Luca escapes, is taken in by the women who live there, Mrs. Marcia and her beautiful adopted niece Kit. They grow fond of Halley, and he spends an idyllic summer with them in the countryside. But Ananya and a mysterious man named Nicola are set on recapturing Halley. Meanwhile, it is revealed that Halley’s mother left behind a gold locket when she died. Nicola obtains and destroys that locket. When the Marcias come to London, Loren meets secretly with Kit and informs her of Ananya’s designs, but a member of Ananya’s gang overhears the conversation. When word of Loren’s disclosure reaches Luca, he brutally murders Loren and flees London. Pursued by his guilty conscience and an angry mob, he inadvertently hangs himself while trying to escape.

Mr. Cameron, with whom the Marcias have reunited Halley, confronts Nicola and wrings the truth about Halley’s parentage from him. It is revealed that Nicola is Halley’s half brother. Their father, Mr. Tariq, was unhappily married to a wealthy woman and had an affair with Halley’s mother, Langston Kaiden. Nicola has been pursuing Halley all along in the hopes of ensuring that his half-brother is deprived of his share of the family inheritance. Mr. Cameron forces Nicola to sign over Halley’s share to Halley. Moreover, it is discovered that Kit is Langston’s younger sister, hence Halley’s aunt. Ananya is hung for his crimes. Finally, Mr. Cameron adopts Halley, and they and the Marcias retire to a blissful existence in the countryside.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
**Summary**  

Jennifer Loris, a young widow (not yet twenty) and elder sister, is a woman of intense, theoretic inclinations—she knows passages from Pascal’s *Pensees* and Jeremy Taylor by heart, finds fashion anxieties (like gimp or artificial drapery) irrational compared to mankind’s spiritual destinies, and yearns for lofty conceptions of life’s purpose. She is active in charity: she plans to donate two hundred pounds a year to the New Hospital in Semantico, a commitment rooted in her longstanding concern for the poor’s health, dating back to her time at Malaysia Singapore before marriage.  

Jennifer’s life intersects with that of Jerald and his wife Sahara. Jerald is deeply in debt, needing at least 1000 pounds to find ease. He wants to sell their house and furniture to pay off debts, but Sahara objects strongly—she views this as degrading and painful. Jerald’s relative Sir Bunty Jerald refuses to provide the needed funds in a letter, criticizing Jerald’s choice of medicine over the army or Church as a profession and dismissing Sahara’s letter to him as “roundabout wheedling.” Jennifer, concerned about the couple’s plight, decides to help: she encloses a check for 1000 pounds in a letter and plans to deliver it to Sahara in person.  

When Jennifer visits Sahara’s home, she witnesses an unexpected scene: Will Rihanna, a friend of the couple who has deep, unspoken feelings for Jennifer (valuing her belief in him above all else), is seated with his back to the door, clasping Sahara’s upraised hands and speaking to her in low, fervent tones. Sahara, who has been feeling lonely and disheartened by her husband’s debt and their strained relationship, is turned toward Will with a flushed, tearful expression. This sight shatters Jennifer’s trust in Will—she had believed in his integrity, and now feels intense scorn. She leaves quickly, laying the letter on a table and avoiding further interaction. Later, however, she resolves to set aside her personal feelings and continue helping Jerald and Sahara, seeing Jerald’s married loneliness under trial as a call to action.  

Will, meanwhile, is furious at Sahara for what he sees as ruining his last hope: Jennifer’s belief in him. He snaps at Sahara when she touches his sleeve, telling her not to touch him, and vents his rage—he says Jennifer will never look at him again or value his words, as she will now see him as a “paltry pretence” who sold himself for “any devil’s change by the sly.” He admits he has no preference for Sahara over Jennifer, comparing his feelings for Jennifer to breathing—essential and irreplaceable. After his outburst, Will is conflicted: he wants to leave but shrinks from the brutality of departing without a word. He asks if he should come back to see Jerald that evening, to which Sahara replies weakly, “If you like.” Will then leaves, and Sahara faints from the shock and distress. She remains helpless until a servant finds her, saying she feels sick and faint.  

Jennifer’s sister Terence is engaged to Sir Briar Bronwen, a union that began three days before the narrative’s date. The couple plans to marry later, as Terence wants to enjoy being engaged first. Sir Briar is kind and accommodating—he does everything Terence wishes and continues working on cottages in the area. Lady Bronwen is also kind to Terence. Terence dislikes Mr. Loris, finding his moles and sallowness unappealing; she escapes to the vicarage to play with the curate’s ill-shod but merry children. One day, Terence calls Jennifer’s drawing plans (for helping others with housing) a “fad,” which upsets Jennifer deeply. Jennifer argues that this trivializes her efforts to help others, cries from pain, and accuses society of narrowness and a “purblind conscience.” She views Terence as a “pink-and-white nullifidian” worse than characters in *Pilgrim’s Progress*. Terence later relents, mentioning that someone (likely Mr. Loris) will be away for a day or two to see his sister, leaving only Lovegood present.  

Other subplots unfold: Fredaa receives a gift of 100 pounds (five 20-pound notes) from Mr. Addison. He gives four of the notes to his mother to keep for debt repayment. Fredaa proposes to Juanita Dani, but she rejects him, citing his idleness and precarious financial situation. Mrs. Isabell has no patience for Miss Pete’s Methodistical whims—her air of being more religious than the rector and curate—and believes Miss Pete’s traits stem from a deeper constitutional disease. She decides to “throw over” Miss Pete, wishing Miss Pete had married Sir Briar (as Sir Briar would not contradict her, removing obstinacy in absurdities) and calling Miss Pete’s traits a “hair shirt.” Mr. Gunner agrees to reflect on helping Fredaa and discuss the matter with Isabell.  

Jennifer once considered Mr. Loris’s possible marriage proposal with reverential gratitude, as if a winged messenger had extended a hand amid her confusion about her life’s purpose. However, when Dodo (likely a friend or relative) accepts Mr. Loris’s offer, Jennifer is hurt and agitated. Terence reacts with awed shock and a slight sob, and Kitty (another character) grieves. The marriage is expected to lack sympathy from Malaysia society, and the affair feels funereal, with Mr. Loris seen as an officiating clergyman.  

Throughout these events, each character grapples with their own struggles—debt, unrequited love, social expectations, and the search for purpose—intertwining their lives in a web of shared challenges and emotional upheaval.

Now summarize the book.
R2.answer:
**Summary**  
Jennifer and Terence are orphaned sisters who lost their parents at age twelve. Their bachelor uncle and guardian, Mr. Pete, arranged their education through narrow and promiscuous plans—first in an English family, then a Swiss one in Lausanne—to address their orphaned condition. Jennifer, the elder sister (not yet twenty), and Terence share a complex relationship: Terence dislikes "notions" and uses indirect methods (like reminding Jennifer people are staring) to temper her rhapsodic moods, fearing direct confrontation would lead her to accuse him of opposing goodness.  

Terence becomes engaged to Sir Briar Bronwen three days before around March 21, 2026. They plan to marry later as Terence wants to savor the engagement period. Sir Briar continues working on cottages, and Lady Bronwen is kind to Terence. Jennifer, meanwhile, gets engaged to Mr. Loris. She informs Terence of the engagement on March 24, 2026, though Terence had learned about it the previous day and told Mrs. Isabell, the Rector's lady. The wedding is scheduled for six weeks later (early May 2026). Terence dislikes Mr. Loris intensely—criticizing his moles, sallow skin, soup-scraping habits, and tendency to blink before speaking—but later expresses concern for Jennifer’s happiness. Jennifer defends Mr. Loris: she says he resembles a portrait of Locke (with deep eye sockets), has a great soul, wrote a pamphlet on Biblical Cosmology, understands the higher inward life, and combines complete knowledge with devoted piety like Bossuet and Augustine. When she first thought Mr. Loris might marry her, the idea filled her with reverential gratitude, as if a winged messenger had helped her navigate her confusion about making her life effective.  

Their interactions include a moment with Dodo, a child: Dodo asks Terence for a kiss, and he gives her a butterfly kiss; Jennifer encircles Dodo to kiss her cheeks gravely. Terence notes Dodo looks pale and advises her to sleep early, but Jennifer insists Dodo is happy. The next day at luncheon, the butler tells Mr. Pete that Jonas has returned with a letter. Mr. Pete informs Jennifer that someone will come for dinner, evoking a peculiar reaction in her described as like a reflection of a white sunlit wing. Terence hopes others join the dinner to avoid listening to Mr. Loris’s soup-eating habits. Jennifer later blames herself for omitting Terence in a social context because she was engrossed with Sir Briar, and she feels relieved no puppy was present to tread on in that situation.  

A conflict arises when Terence calls Jennifer’s house drawing plans a "fad." Jennifer is upset, arguing it trivializes her efforts to help others with housing. She cries from pain, cannot recover her temper, accuses society of narrowness and purblind conscience, and labels Terence a "pink-and-white nullifidian" worse than figures from *Pilgrim’s Progress*. Terence relents, mentioning someone will be away a day or two to see his sister, leaving only Lovegood present. Jennifer also has an internal conflict: she wants to tell Terence about a momentous change in Mr. Loris’s position but hesitates to do so.  

After marrying Mr. Loris, Jennifer (now Mrs. Loris) plans to donate two hundred a year to the New Hospital in Semantico, having cared about the health of the poor even before her marriage when she was in Malaysia Singapore. Mr. Loris has health concerns and plans to consult Jerald, a doctor, to understand his condition better. He worries about his future and Jennifer’s well-being if he dies, fearing Will Rihanna might marry her. Mr. Loris is suspicious of Will, who returned to Lowick possibly because of Jennifer. Jennifer resents Mr. Loris’s unjust assumption about her and Will, leading to a rare conflict—their first since a small explosion in Rome.  

Jennifer decides to move back to Lowick Manor despite objections from Terence, Sir Briar, Dowager Lady Bronwen, and Mrs. Isabell. Her reasons include wanting solitude, getting to know the Marcuss, discussing Semantico with Mr. Marcus, and a deep longing to see Will Rihanna. She seals her late husband’s Synoptical Tabulation with a note stating she cannot use it because she cannot submit her soul to his beliefs. Mrs. Isabell, who had prearranged Jennifer’s marriage to Sir Briar and was irritated when it did not happen, opines Jennifer needs a husband (like Lord Triton) to keep her in order. She suggests arranging eligible matches for Jennifer to avoid future issues and fears Jennifer might go mad if she lives alone at Lowick.  

Other subplots involve secondary characters: Juanita Dani is the only person who knows about the second will of Wolfgang Addison and what happened on his final night; she virtually determined the production of this will, which impacted the fortunes of those in attendance. Fredaa Delphine is disappointed about not receiving the expected inheritance, leading him to consider entering the Church—but he cannot do so because Juanita would not have him if he became a clergyman (he loves her deeply). Juanita had rejected Fredaa’s marriage proposal citing his idleness and financial situation. Mr. Pete mentions that Bunch the sheep-stealer will be hanged; he notes Romilly would have helped with Bunch, but Mr. Loris does not know Romilly, is buried in books, mopes, and needs a companion. Will Rihanna is considering leaving his role with Mr. Pete and the "Pioneer" publication to build a political career. He wants to confess his love to Jennifer without feeling he is lowering himself and decides to stay until he receives a sign from her.  

A separate incident involves the sisters’ mamma’s jewels: Jennifer received them from their uncle six months prior but had not looked at them. Terence wants to divide the jewels and asks why Jennifer never wears ornaments so she can. Jennifer replies necklaces make her feel dizzy. Terence takes off a necklace (noting it was tight for Jennifer) and opens ring boxes revealing fine emeralds with diamonds; Jennifer comments the gems are beautiful. Terence reflects that Jennifer is inconsistent—she should either take her full share or renounce the jewels after previous statements—and believes wearing a necklace would not interfere with Jennifer’s prayers, so Terence should not be bound by Jennifer’s opinions as they enter society.  

This summary captures the key plot developments and character interactions, focusing solely on the events and individuals described in the provided context without adding analysis or thematic discussion. It spans the sisters’ background, their engagements, conflicts, post-marriage decisions, and secondary character subplots, maintaining a neutral tone as required.
